title: Dune
author: Frank Herbert
description: A sweeping tale of desert planets, political intrigue, and ecological mysticism. The book that defined modern science fiction and inspired everything from Star Wars to Avatar.

1.review: A masterpiece. Every sci-fi trope traces back here. Read it.

2.review: Great world, glacial pace. Worth it for the lore, but I needed coffee.

3.review: The political intrigue alone earns a 4.5. Ecological systems thinking ahead of its time.

4.review: Overrated but in the best possible way. Classic for a reason.

5.review: Started it three times before I finished it. Beautiful once it clicked.

title: The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo
author: Taylor Jenkins Reid
description: A reclusive Hollywood icon finally tells her story to an unknown journalist. A dazzling, addictive saga of ambition, identity, and love across a glittering life of secrets.

1.review: Entertaining but I saw every twist coming. Good beach read though.

2.review: A perfect novel. I sobbed. I gasped. I called my mother.

3.review: Structure was immaculate. Could have used 20% more ambiguity.

4.review: This is what I mean when I say popular can also be good.

5.review: The theatrical quality of her life! Evelyn Hugo is a legend.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
description: A lone astronaut wakes up with no memory on a desperate mission to save Earth. Funny, heartfelt, and stuffed with real science. The most joyful book about humanity's extinction you'll ever read.

1.review: Rocky. That's all. Rocky made me feel things I didn't expect from a physics textbook.

2.review: The science stuff went over my head but the heart of it was undeniable.

3.review: Weir made me care about astrophage. ASTROPHAGE. 5 stars.

4.review: Fun, breezy. A little too neat for my taste. The friendship was sweet.

5.review: I laughed out loud. In a book about the apocalypse. Remarkable.

title: Piranesi
author: Susanna Clarke
description: A man lives in a labyrinthine House whose halls contain infinite statues and oceans. He knows almost nothing, including who he really is. A novel of extraordinary strangeness and quiet devastation.

1.review: The most unique reading experience in years. Clarke is operating on another level.

2.review: I didn't fully understand it but I felt it in my chest. That counts.

3.review: Beautifully written but I wanted more resolution. Too dreamlike for me.

4.review: My pick. My masterpiece. The House is real and it has tides.

5.review: Short, strange, and unforgettable. I thought about it for a week.

title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow
author: Gabrielle Zevin
description: Two friends build video games together across decades. A novel about creativity, love, loss, and what it means to make something that outlasts you. Also the best book about friendship written in years.

1.review: The gaming details were perfect. The emotional core even more so.

2.review: I wept at the end. Beautiful meditation on creative partnership.

3.review: The game development accuracy alone earns extra points. Rare authenticity.

4.review: Good but I found Sam frustrating in all the wrong ways.

5.review: My pick. Vindicated. This is the novel of our generation.

title: The Midnight Library
author: Matt Haig
description: Between life and death lies a library containing every version of your life you could have lived. Nora Seed has the chance to undo her regrets. But which life is the right one?

1.review: Cozy philosophy wrapped in a warm blanket. I needed this one.

2.review: The message hit me exactly when I needed it to.

3.review: The concept was stronger than the execution. But I smiled at the end.

4.review: Inspiring premise. Very predictable arc. I've seen this movie.

5.review: Accessible and warm. The Haig earnestness lands more often than not.

title: Mexican Gothic
author: Silvia Moreno-Garcia
description: 1950s Mexico. A glamorous socialite investigates her cousin's mysterious illness at a remote mansion run by a deeply unsettling English family. Gothic horror with a feminist, colonial critique at its heart.

1.review: The atmosphere alone is worth five stars. Deeply unsettling in the best way.

2.review: Noemi is a fantastic protagonist. The ending got weird but I was along for the ride.

3.review: Elegant prose but the horror mechanics felt a bit genre-by-numbers to me.

4.review: The colonial horror is not subtle and shouldn't be.

5.review: The creep factor built perfectly. That house is evil and I loved every page.
